description: ReadyMCAT teach-on-miss ladders. Pre-authored guiding sub-questions for high-value MCAT concepts drawn from the highest-weight AAMC content categories (see taxonomy.json). When a card carrying one of a concept's match_tags is graded 'Again', the desktop reviewer runs that concept's ladder one rung at a time (attempt -> reveal sub-answer -> self-mark Got it / Missed) before re-showing the main question.

schema.concepts[].title: human-readable concept name (shown in the reviewer)
schema.concepts[].category: AAMC content category id from taxonomy.json (informational)
schema.concepts[].match_tags: list of tag prefixes; a card matches if any of its tags equals or is path-prefixed (on '::') by one of these. Longest matching prefix across all concepts wins.
schema.concepts[].resource: {label, url} surfaced as a 'needs content review' nudge if the main question is missed again after the ladder (falls back to the card's own embedded link when present)
schema.concepts[].ladder: ordered list of {q, a} rungs (2-3); q = guiding sub-question shown first, a = sub-answer revealed after the student attempts it

ladder.q: Glycolysis has two phases. In the energy-INVESTMENT phase, how many ATP are consumed, and at which two enzyme steps?
ladder.a: 2 ATP invested - one at hexokinase (glucose -> glucose-6-phosphate) and one at phosphofructokinase-1 (F6P -> fructose-1,6-bisphosphate).

ladder.q: In the energy-PAYOFF phase, how many ATP and NADH are produced per glucose (recall each glucose becomes two 3-carbon units)?
ladder.a: 4 ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation (2 at phosphoglycerate kinase, 2 at pyruvate kinase) and 2 NADH (at glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase).

ladder.q: So what is the NET yield per glucose, and what are the carbon end-products?
ladder.a: Net 2 ATP + 2 NADH + 2 pyruvate per glucose.

concepts.title: Citric acid cycle: inputs and per-turn yield

ladder.q: What molecule actually enters the citric acid cycle (it is not pyruvate itself), and how is it formed from pyruvate?
ladder.a: Acetyl-CoA. Pyruvate dehydrogenase oxidatively decarboxylates pyruvate, releasing 1 CO2 and producing 1 NADH per pyruvate.

ladder.q: Per ONE turn of the cycle (per acetyl-CoA), how many NADH, FADH2, and GTP are produced?
ladder.a: 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, and 1 GTP (or ATP) per acetyl-CoA, with 2 CO2 released.

ladder.q: Per molecule of GLUCOSE (which yields 2 acetyl-CoA), what is the cycle's total output?
ladder.a: Double it: 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, 2 GTP, and 4 CO2 per glucose.

ladder.q: In the electron transport chain, what is the FINAL electron acceptor and what product forms?
ladder.a: Oxygen (O2) is the final electron acceptor; it is reduced to water (H2O).

ladder.q: As electrons pass through the complexes, what gradient is built, and across which membrane?
ladder.a: Protons (H+) are pumped from the matrix into the intermembrane space, building an electrochemical proton gradient across the INNER mitochondrial membrane (the proton-motive force).

ladder.q: How is that gradient used to make ATP, and what is this coupling called?
ladder.a: Protons flow back into the matrix through ATP synthase, driving ATP synthesis. Coupling the oxidation-driven gradient to ATP synthesis is chemiosmosis (oxidative phosphorylation).

ladder.q: What does Vmax represent, and what physically limits it?
ladder.a: Vmax is the maximum rate reached when the enzyme is saturated with substrate; it is limited by the total enzyme concentration (Vmax = kcat[E]total).

ladder.q: What does Km represent operationally, and what does a LOW Km indicate?
ladder.a: Km is the substrate concentration at which the rate is half of Vmax. A low Km means high apparent affinity (little substrate needed to reach half-max).

ladder.q: On a Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) plot, what do the y- and x-intercepts give you?
ladder.a: y-intercept = 1/Vmax; x-intercept = -1/Km (slope = Km/Vmax).

ladder.q: A COMPETITIVE inhibitor binds where, and how does it change apparent Km and Vmax?
ladder.a: It binds the active site, competing with substrate: apparent Km INCREASES, Vmax is UNCHANGED (it can be overcome by adding more substrate).

ladder.q: A pure NONCOMPETITIVE inhibitor binds where, and how does it change Km and Vmax?
ladder.a: It binds an allosteric site (equally to E and ES), so Vmax DECREASES and Km is UNCHANGED (cannot be overcome by more substrate).

ladder.q: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, where do the lines intersect for each?
ladder.a: Competitive: lines meet on the y-axis (same Vmax). Noncompetitive: lines meet on the x-axis (same Km).

concepts.title: Amino acid acid-base behavior & isoelectric point

ladder.q: At physiological pH (~7.4), what is the charge on the backbone carboxyl group and the amino group of a free amino acid?
ladder.a: Carboxyl (pKa ~2) is deprotonated -COO- (negative); amino (pKa ~9-10) is protonated -NH3+ (positive). The result is a zwitterion with net charge ~0.

ladder.q: What is the isoelectric point (pI), and how do you compute it for an amino acid with a NON-ionizable side chain?
ladder.a: pI is the pH at which net charge = 0. For a neutral side chain, pI = average of the two backbone pKa values: (pKa_COOH + pKa_NH3+)/2.

ladder.q: For an ACIDIC amino acid (e.g., aspartate, three pKa's), which two pKa's do you average?
ladder.a: Average the two LOWEST pKa's (the pair flanking the neutral/zwitterionic form) -> a low pI.

ladder.q: DNA replication is 'semiconservative' - what exactly does that mean for each daughter duplex?
ladder.a: Each daughter double helix keeps one parental (template) strand and one newly synthesized strand.

ladder.q: DNA polymerase only synthesizes 5'->3'. What does that force to happen at the replication fork?
ladder.a: One new strand (leading) is made continuously toward the fork; the other (lagging) is made discontinuously as Okazaki fragments away from the fork.

ladder.q: Name the enzyme that unwinds the helix and the enzyme that joins Okazaki fragments.
ladder.a: Helicase unwinds the duplex; DNA ligase seals the nicks between Okazaki fragments (primase lays RNA primers; topoisomerase relieves supercoiling).

ladder.q: What enzyme carries out transcription, and in which directions does it read the template and build the RNA?
ladder.a: RNA polymerase reads the template strand 3'->5' and synthesizes RNA 5'->3'. No primer is required.

ladder.q: Name the three main eukaryotic pre-mRNA processing steps before the transcript leaves the nucleus.
ladder.a: Addition of a 5' 7-methylguanosine cap, addition of a 3' poly-A tail, and splicing out of introns (joining exons).

ladder.q: The mature mRNA sequence matches which DNA strand (apart from U replacing T)?
ladder.a: The coding (sense) strand. mRNA is complementary to the template strand, so it reads like the coding strand with U in place of T.

ladder.q: State the two Hardy-Weinberg equations and what p and q represent.
ladder.a: p + q = 1 (allele frequencies) and p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1 (genotype frequencies), where p = dominant allele frequency and q = recessive allele frequency.

ladder.q: If 1 in 100 individuals shows the recessive phenotype, find q and then p.
What fraction of that population are heterozygous carriers?
What ion movement drives the DEPOLARIZATION (rising) phase of the action potential?
ladder.a: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open and Na+ rushes INTO the cell, driving the membrane potential sharply positive (toward ~+30-40 mV).

ladder.q: What causes repolarization, and then the brief hyperpolarization that follows?
ladder.a: Na+ channels inactivate while voltage-gated K+ channels open, so K+ flows OUT (repolarization); the slow closing of K+ channels overshoots, causing transient hyperpolarization.

ladder.q: What is the absolute refractory period, and why does it make propagation one-way?
ladder.a: It is the interval when Na+ channels are inactivated and cannot reopen at any stimulus strength; it prevents the impulse from traveling backward, so action potentials propagate unidirectionally.

ladder.q: Trace the normal electrical conduction pathway of the heart from where the signal originates.
ladder.a: SA node -> AV node -> bundle of His -> right and left bundle branches -> Purkinje fibers.

ladder.q: Why is the conduction DELAY at the AV node functionally important?
ladder.a: It lets the atria finish contracting and fully fill the ventricles before the ventricles contract, optimizing stroke volume.

ladder.q: Which structure is the normal pacemaker and why does it set the heart rate?
ladder.a: The SA node - it has the fastest intrinsic rate of spontaneous depolarization, so it entrains (paces) the rest of the conduction system.

ladder.q: Name the basic renal processes that determine what ends up in the urine.
ladder.a: Filtration (at the glomerulus), reabsorption, and secretion (along the tubules), then excretion. Urine = filtered - reabsorbed + secreted.

ladder.q: Contrast the DESCENDING vs ASCENDING limb of the loop of Henle in terms of what they move.
ladder.a: Descending limb is permeable to water (water leaves, solutes stay) -> filtrate concentrates. Ascending limb is impermeable to water but actively pumps out Na+/K+/Cl- (solutes leave) -> filtrate dilutes.

ladder.q: Which hormone makes the collecting duct permeable to water, and what is the effect on urine?
ladder.a: ADH (vasopressin) inserts aquaporins in the collecting duct -> more water reabsorbed -> concentrated urine. (Aldosterone increases Na+ reabsorption distally.)

ladder.q: Write the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a weak-acid buffer.
ladder.a: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]) - pH equals pKa plus the log of conjugate base over weak acid.

ladder.q: A buffer is most effective (and pH = pKa) when what is true of [A-] and [HA]?
ladder.a: When [A-] = [HA]; then log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa. This is the center of the effective buffering region (about pKa +/- 1).

ladder.q: At the HALF-equivalence point of a weak-acid titration, what does pH equal?
ladder.a: pH = pKa, because exactly half the weak acid has been converted to its conjugate base ([A-] = [HA]).

ladder.q: State Le Chatelier's principle in one sentence.
ladder.a: When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance.

ladder.q: For an EXOTHERMIC reaction, which way does equilibrium shift if you raise the temperature?
ladder.a: Toward the reactants (left). Heat behaves like a product, so adding heat shifts away from products and K decreases.

ladder.q: For a gas-phase equilibrium, what happens if you decrease the volume (increase pressure)?
ladder.a: It shifts toward the side with FEWER moles of gas to relieve the pressure.

ladder.q: For rate = k[A]^m[B]^n, what are m and n called, and can you read them off the balanced equation?
ladder.a: They are the reaction orders in A and B (overall order = m + n). They are determined EXPERIMENTALLY, not from stoichiometric coefficients.

ladder.q: If doubling [A] quadruples the rate, what is the order with respect to A?
ladder.a: Second order (2^2 = 4). (A 2x rate would be first order; no change would be zero order.)

ladder.q: How does a catalyst speed a reaction, and does it move the equilibrium?
ladder.a: It lowers the activation energy via an alternate pathway, speeding the forward and reverse rates equally. It does NOT change deltaG, K, or the equilibrium position.

ladder.q: Write the ideal gas law and define every variable (with units of T).
ladder.a: PV = nRT: P pressure, V volume, n moles, R the gas constant, T absolute temperature in KELVIN.

ladder.q: At constant T and n, how are P and V related (whose law), and how are V and T related at constant P?
ladder.a: P and V are inversely related: P1V1 = P2V2 (Boyle's law). At constant P, V is directly proportional to T (Charles's law).

ladder.q: Under what conditions do REAL gases deviate most from ideal behavior, and why?
ladder.a: High pressure and low temperature: molecules are close together, so their finite volume and intermolecular attractions become significant.

ladder.q: What is the difference between enantiomers and diastereomers?
ladder.a: Enantiomers are non-superimposable mirror images (opposite configuration at EVERY chiral center). Diastereomers are stereoisomers that are NOT mirror images (differ at some but not all chiral centers).

ladder.q: For a molecule with n chiral centers, what is the maximum number of stereoisomers?
ladder.a: 2^n (fewer if internal symmetry creates meso compounds).

ladder.q: How do a pair of enantiomers differ in measurable properties?
ladder.a: Identical ordinary physical properties EXCEPT they rotate plane-polarized light equally but in OPPOSITE directions (optical activity), and they behave differently in chiral environments.

ladder.q: Write the Gibbs free energy relationship among deltaG, deltaH, and deltaS.
ladder.a: deltaG = deltaH - T*deltaS (with T in Kelvin).

ladder.q: What sign of deltaG marks a spontaneous (thermodynamically favorable) process?
ladder.a: deltaG < 0 is spontaneous/exergonic; deltaG > 0 is nonspontaneous; deltaG = 0 is at equilibrium.

ladder.q: If deltaH > 0 and deltaS > 0, when is the reaction spontaneous?
ladder.a: At HIGH temperature - a large T makes T*deltaS exceed deltaH, so deltaG becomes negative (entropy-driven).

ladder.q: Write the thin-lens / mirror equation relating focal length to object and image distance.
ladder.a: 1/f = 1/do + 1/di, where f is focal length, do object distance, di image distance.

ladder.q: For a converging lens, what do a POSITIVE vs NEGATIVE image distance (di) tell you about the image?
ladder.a: Positive di -> real, inverted image on the far side. Negative di -> virtual, upright image on the same side as the object.

ladder.q: How is magnification computed, and what does a negative m mean?
ladder.a: m = -di/do = hi/ho. m < 0 means an inverted (real) image; |m| > 1 means enlarged.

ladder.q: State Ohm's law.
ladder.a: V = IR (voltage = current x resistance).

ladder.q: How do resistors combine in SERIES versus in PARALLEL?
ladder.a: Series: R_total = R1 + R2 + ... (sum). Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ..., so R_total is LESS than the smallest resistor.

ladder.q: In a series circuit what quantity is the same everywhere; in a parallel circuit what quantity is the same?
ladder.a: Series: the same CURRENT flows through every element (voltage divides). Parallel: the same VOLTAGE is across each branch (current divides).

ladder.q: Name the three sequential stages in the information-processing model of memory.
ladder.a: Encoding -> storage -> retrieval.

ladder.q: In the Atkinson-Shiffrin (modal) model, what are the three stores, and roughly their capacity/duration?
ladder.a: Sensory memory (large, < ~1 s), short-term/working memory (~7 +/- 2 items, ~15-30 s), and long-term memory (effectively unlimited, durable).

ladder.q: Distinguish explicit (declarative) from implicit (nondeclarative) long-term memory.
ladder.a: Explicit = conscious facts and events (semantic and episodic). Implicit = unconscious skills and conditioned responses (procedural memory, priming).

ladder.q: What did Asch's line-judgment studies demonstrate about conformity?
ladder.a: People often conform to a clearly incorrect majority (normative social influence - the desire to fit in and avoid disapproval), even against the evidence of their own senses.

ladder.q: What did Milgram's studies demonstrate, and what is the core concept?
ladder.a: Ordinary people will obey an authority figure to deliver apparent harm - obedience to authority. Proximity of the authority and of the victim strongly modulate it.

ladder.q: Distinguish normative from informational social influence.
ladder.a: Normative: conform to be liked/accepted -> public compliance. Informational: conform because you believe others have better information -> private acceptance, common in ambiguous situations.

ladder.q: In operant conditioning, distinguish reinforcement from punishment, and 'positive' from 'negative'.
ladder.a: Reinforcement increases a behavior; punishment decreases it. 'Positive' = ADD a stimulus; 'negative' = REMOVE a stimulus.

ladder.q: Give an example of NEGATIVE reinforcement and explain why it is reinforcement, not punishment.
ladder.a: Taking aspirin to remove a headache: the relief (removing an aversive stimulus) makes you more likely to take aspirin again. Because it INCREASES the behavior, it is reinforcement.

ladder.q: Which reinforcement schedule yields the highest, most extinction-resistant response rate?
ladder.a: Variable-ratio (reward after an unpredictable number of responses), e.g., a slot machine.

ladder.q: What sequence does the James-Lange theory propose?
ladder.a: Stimulus -> physiological/bodily arousal -> the emotion IS our interpretation of that bodily response ('I tremble, therefore I am afraid').

ladder.q: How does the Cannon-Bard theory differ from James-Lange?
ladder.a: Arousal and the conscious emotion occur SIMULTANEOUSLY and independently - the stimulus triggers both at once.

ladder.q: What does the Schachter-Singer (two-factor) theory add on top of arousal?
ladder.a: Emotion = physiological arousal PLUS a cognitive label/appraisal of that arousal from context; identical arousal can be labeled as different emotions depending on context.
